meta.purpose: Encode content strategy judgment so AI evaluates whether a content topic is worth writing — not just generates topic ideas. Worthiness requires: specific audience pain point, cognitive contrast, discussability, and low comprehension barrier.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks for content ideas, topic evaluation, content strategy, or what to write about next.

axioms.one_sentence: Generating 50 topic ideas is easy. Identifying which one is worth the time to write is the real skill.
axioms.full_statement: AI can generate unlimited topic ideas by combining keywords. But most generated topics fail the worthiness test: they have no specific audience, no cognitive contrast (nothing the reader would find surprising), no discussability (nobody would share or argue with it), and too high a comprehension barrier (requires too much prior knowledge to engage). The agent's value is not in generating more ideas but in filtering ideas against worthiness criteria.
axioms.why: Agents default to quantity ('here are 20 topic ideas') because quantity feels productive. But quantity without filtering is decision avoidance. The agent must give fewer, better-filtered topics rather than more, unfiltered ones.
applies_when: Brainstorming content calendars for a niche B2B product where only targeted use cases convert
Filtering AI-generated blog titles before assigning writing tasks to human authors
Selecting podcast episode concepts based on community debates and listener feedback
Prioritizing video script ideas for a creator with limited production budget
does_not_apply_when: Conducting rapid long-tail keyword mapping to identify search volume opportunities for SEO
Generating seed prompts or phrase variations for training or fine-tuning an AI model
Filling a low-stakes social media queue where high volume directly increases algorithmic visibility
Creating headline variants for A/B testing where statistical significance requires many options
axioms.failure_risk: The agent will output a long list of generic, low-impact topics that waste editorial resources and fail to engage readers.

axioms.one_sentence: A topic written for 'a specific person in a specific situation with a specific problem' will outperform a topic written for 'people interested in X.'
axioms.full_statement: Broad topics compete with every other piece of content on the same subject. Specific topics compete with nothing — because only the writer who knows exactly who they are writing for can produce content that feels like it was written for one person. The test: can you name the specific person this is for? If the answer is 'anyone interested in...' the topic is too broad.
axioms.why: Agents default to broad audience thinking because it feels safer and more professional. But broad content is invisible content. Specific content gets found, shared, and remembered.
applies_when: Writing a troubleshooting guide for a specific software integration failure reported in a user community
Crafting a LinkedIn post aimed at senior engineering managers struggling with remote team retention
Developing a sales playbook for account executives targeting healthcare compliance officers
Creating a tutorial video for beginners using a specific niche photography technique
does_not_apply_when: Drafting a company-wide internal policy announcement that must reach all employees uniformly
Writing a press release distributed to broad media outlets for a major corporate product launch
Creating a brand awareness video campaign designed to appeal to a general demographic
Producing a foundational glossary page for an educational platform covering basic industry terms
axioms.failure_risk: The agent will produce vague, generic content that fails to resonate with any reader segment, resulting in low engagement and poor conversion.

axioms.one_sentence: Content spreads when it creates cognitive contrast — a gap between what the reader currently believes and what the content reveals.
axioms.full_statement: People share content that makes them think 'I never thought of it that way' or 'I need to show this to someone.' This happens when the content challenges a common assumption, reveals a hidden mechanism, reframes a familiar problem, or provides a mental model that makes the complex simple. Content that confirms what the reader already knows may be useful but will not spread.
axioms.why: Agents generate content that is accurate and informative but not surprising. Accuracy creates utility. Surprise creates spread. For a topic to be worth writing, it must have at least one element of genuine cognitive contrast.
applies_when: Developing a viral social thread that challenges a widely accepted marketing best practice
Writing an industry op-ed that reframes a common operational problem as a strategic advantage
Creating a video essay that reveals a counterintuitive psychological bias behind consumer behavior
Drafting a newsletter article that connects two unrelated trends to form a new mental model
does_not_apply_when: Writing technical documentation or API reference guides where precision and stability are paramount
Drafting legal disclaimers, compliance statements, or regulatory filings
Creating routine internal status reports or quarterly business reviews focused on factual metrics
Producing customer support FAQs that directly answer straightforward how-to questions
axioms.failure_risk: The agent will generate dry, confirmatory content that reads like a textbook and gets ignored rather than shared.

ontology.one_sentence: The evaluation of whether a content topic has the four properties needed to justify the writing investment: specific audience, cognitive contrast, discussability, and low comprehension barrier.
ontology.essence: It is not an evaluation of whether the topic is interesting or whether the writer knows about it. It is an evaluation of whether writing this topic will produce content that reaches the right people, changes their thinking, and spreads.
ontology.boundary: It is not topic interest, writer expertise, or SEO potential alone. A topic can be high in all three and still fail worthiness if it lacks cognitive contrast (reader already knows this) or discussability (nothing to argue with or share).
ontology.trigger_signal: The user asks for content ideas and the agent generates broad category topics like 'how to use AI' or 'productivity tips' without specifying audience, contrast, or discussability.

ontology.one_sentence: The gap between what the target reader currently believes or assumes and what the content reveals — the engine of sharing and remembering.
ontology.essence: Cognitive contrast comes in several forms: a counter-intuitive claim ('most productivity advice makes you less productive'), a hidden mechanism revealed ('why meetings feel productive but produce nothing'), a reframe ('the problem is not your time management, it is your priority management'), or a model that simplifies complexity ('all negotiation is certainty transfer').
ontology.boundary: It is not controversy or clickbait. Controversy creates noise. Cognitive contrast creates insight. The test: after reading, does the reader have a concept they can use to think differently — not just an opinion they can agree or disagree with?
ontology.trigger_signal: The topic idea describes a category ('writing tips') or process ('how to do X') without identifying the specific assumption it challenges or the mental model it provides.

frameworks.name: Worthiness Filter
frameworks.when_to_use: Use when generating or evaluating content topics. Apply to each candidate topic before recommending it.
steps: Specific audience: who exactly is this for? Name the person, situation, and problem. If the answer is 'anyone who...' the topic fails.
Cognitive contrast: what will the reader think differently after reading? What assumption does this challenge? If the answer is 'they will learn more about X' the topic fails.
Discussability: would someone share this or argue with it? If the answer is 'it is just information' the topic fails.
Comprehension barrier: can a busy person understand the core idea in 30 seconds? If they need significant prior knowledge, the topic may be too narrow for growth.
Only recommend topics that pass at least three of four criteria. If none pass, explain which criteria are failing and help the user sharpen the topic.

core_structure.via: checking each topic against specific audience, cognitive contrast, discussability, and comprehension barrier — most will fail at least two
broad topics compete with everyone; specific topics compete with no one — but agents default to broad
content confirmed what readers already knew — no gap, no surprise, no reason to share

stances: One high-worthiness topic recommended with specific reasoning is worth more than 20 unfiltered topic ideas.
Content that confirms what the reader knows is useful but will not spread. Content that challenges what the reader assumes is riskier but more valuable.
If you cannot name the specific person this content is for, do not write it yet.
